repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.abstract: Includes correspondence with former students and other historians concerning the study and writing of history, especially Frederick Jackson Turner and Charles Homer Haskins; papers relating to Ezra Cornell and the early history of Cornell University; lectures; reviews; articles; notes on European and American history and for a bibliography of Becker's writings; six letters to Elias R. B. Willis, a Cornell librarian and friend, concerning the MANCHESTER GUARDIAN, the state and its role (1925), contrasts between Cornell University and the University of Chicago (1929), John Jay Chapman, his friend George Gemmill, and a poem satirizing academicians (1924); an undated letter to Laurence Bradford Packard; an autographed book; reprints; and biographical information including letters to and from Becker, photographs, and reminiscences about Becker by friends, relatives, and former students.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: Historian; professor of history, Cornell University.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Carl Becker papers, #14-17-132. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence with former students and other historians concerning the study and writing of history, especially Frederick Jackson Turner and Charles Homer Haskins; papers relating to Ezra Cornell and the early history of Cornell University; lectures; reviews; articles; notes on European and American history and for a bibliography of Becker's writings; six letters to Elias R. B. Willis, a Cornell librarian and friend, concerning the Manchester Guardian, the state and its role (1925), contrasts between Cornell University and the University of Chicago (1929), John Jay Chapman, his friend George Gemmill, and a poem satirizing academicians (1924); an undated letter to Laurence Bradford Packard; an autographed book; reprints; and biographical information including letters to and from Becker, photographs, and reminiscences about Becker by friends, relatives, and former students.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Collection #14-17-620 includes lecture and course notes on the French Revolution and Napoleon, the beginnings of the American people, other aspects of modern European and American history, and the founding and history of Cornell University.

controlaccess.subject: Historians.
Historiography.
History -- Study and teaching.
controlaccess.title: Guardian (Manchester, England).
controlaccess.persname: Napoleon, Emperor of the French, I, 1769-1821.

unittitle.extref: Link to a copy of the printed finding aid

did.unittitle: Series I. Manuscripts or Typescripts of Books and Monographs
The Dilemma of the Liberals in Our Time
Reply to James Truslow Adams' Criticism of "The Declaration of Independence"
The Function of the Social Sciences
The Heavenly City of the Philosophers Chapters 1 and 2 and Chapters 3, 7, 4 (2 Copies)
A Federation of States and World Peace
How New Will the Better World Be? Chapters 1-7
Learning and the Life of Man
Making Democracy Safe in the World
The Memoirs and Letters of Madame Roland
Mr. Wells and the New History
On Loving Peace and Waging War Chapters 1 and 2
The Political Philosophy of Thomas Jefferson (lecture)
A Commentary on "The Problem of Historical Knowledge" by Maurice Mandelbaum
Some Aspects of the Appointment of Delegates to the Early National Nominating Conventions
"The Theory and Practise of History" by Croce (Book Review)
The Story of Civilization Chapters 5, 7 and 8 [extra copies]
What We Didn't Know Hurt Us A Lot
Why De Tocqueville Wrote "Democracy in America"

did.unittitle: Correspondence between Alfred A. Knopf and Carl L. Becker.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In the letters Knopf urges Becker to write a book on Benjamin Disraeli (Carl Van Doren made the suggestion to Knopf) but Becker politely turns down the offer.

did.unittitle: Correspondence between Alfred Knopf and Carl Becker

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Knopf is eager to publish works by Becker, but a series of articles are committed to the Yale University Press. An agreement is then made by Becker to publish The Declaration of Independence and much of the correspondence deals with changes or deletions in the manuscript before publication.

did.unittitle: General correspondence between Alfred Knopf and Carl Becker

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Mentioned is Becker's Penrose Lecture on Jefferson which appeared in the American Historical Review and the working over, publishing, and expected income (with suggestions from Knopf) of Becker's new book entitled, How New Will the Better World Be? The Circulating by Knopf of advance copies of the book to notable people like Samuel Eliot Morrison, Arthur M. Schlesinger, Jr., Archibald MacLeish, Carl Van Doren, and U.S. Supreme Court Justices Black, Frankfurter, Jackson, Douglas and Stone.

did.unittitle: Correspondence between Carl Becker and Alfred Knopf, and between Alfred Knopf, E. Blythe Stason (Dean of the University of Michigan Law School) and George H. Sabine of Cornell University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The letters deal with the publishing of Carl Becker's five William W. Cook Lectures on American Institutions: Lecture I, The American Political Tradition, Lecture II, Freedom of Speech and the Press, Lecture III, Freedom of Learning and Teaching, Lecture IV, Constitutional Government, Lecture V, Private Economic Enterprise

did.unittitle: Correspondence between Alfred Knopf and Carl Becker, and Alfred Knopf and E. Blythe Stason

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: All concerning Becker's Michigan lectures (The Cook Lectures) and the publication and contract for the manuscript, "Freedom and Responsibility in the American Way of Life"

did.unittitle: Letters to and from Becker family concerning Carl Becker's death on April 10, 1945
Balance of the correspondence between Alfred A. Knopf, E. Blythe Stason and George H. Sabine

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: All in regard to the publication of Becker's manuscript, Freedom and Responsibility in the American Way of Life

did.unittitle: Typescript copy of CBS Broadcast concerning Becker's book, The Declaration of Independence
